refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies a specific DP) and focuses on the core problem: making spatial features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent verification and design of each capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface properties across the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and locate static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent active work zones and their operational constraints.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Integrate captured spatial features into a unified worksite model accessible to planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints); expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign zone-level feature inventories (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; maintain zone-feature mappings in a relational schema; provide zone-based query and update APIs.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a voxel-grid occupancy model with semantic labels; support incremental updates from streaming sensor data; provide spatial query (collision, proximity, traversability) and export to planning-compatible formats.

DP4: Integrate external worksite blueprints/CAD models as reference geometry; overlay real-time sensor observations to detect deviations and dynamic features; maintain a hybrid static-dynamic feature registry; reconcile conflicts via confidence scoring.

DP5: Establish a feature capture pipeline: raw sensor ingestion → feature detection (rule-based or learned classifiers) → spatial registration → conflict resolution → persistent model store; expose model via standardized query language; support versioning and audit trails for safety compliance.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Establish a feature capture pipeline: raw sensor ingestion → feature detection (rule-based or learned classifiers) → spatial registration → conflict resolution → persistent model store; expose model via standardized query language; support versioning and audit trails for safety compliance.

potential_sources: Feature detection logic tightly coupled to specific sensor modalities (risk: hard to swap sensors or add new modalities without redesign).
Spatial registration assumes a single global coordinate frame; multi-robot or distributed sensing scenarios may require frame negotiation (risk: scalability to heterogeneous deployments).
Conflict resolution heuristics may be domain-specific (e.g., construction vs. mining); embedding them in the pipeline reduces reusability (risk: limited generalization across worksite types).
Versioning and audit trail requirements may impose performance overhead on real-time model updates (risk: latency sensitivity in dynamic environments).
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is selected because it explicitly addresses the acceptance criterion (model generation and availability) and incorporates safety/compliance concerns (versioning, audit). The pipeline structure supports modularity at each stage (ingestion, detection, registration, resolution, storage, query), enabling independent evolution. Coupling risks are manageable through clear interface contracts between stages and parameterization of domain-specific logic.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite features are sufficiently observable via available sensing modalities (vision, range, inertial). Risk: Occluded or GPS-denied environments may require alternative or supplementary data sources (e.g., manual annotation, external maps).

Assumption: Feature detection can be performed with acceptable latency and accuracy for planning/execution timescales. Risk: Learned classifiers may require domain-specific training data; rule-based detectors may miss novel or ambiguous features.

Assumption: A single global coordinate frame is sufficient for the worksite. Risk: Large-scale or multi-zone sites may require hierarchical or local frames; frame drift over time may accumulate errors.

Assumption: Conflict resolution (e.g., sensor disagreement, dynamic feature changes) can be automated via confidence scoring. Risk: Safety-critical features (e.g., hazard zones) may require human validation before acceptance into the model.

Assumption: Downstream planning/execution functions can consume the model via a standardized query interface. Risk: Heterogeneous consumer requirements (e.g., different coordinate conventions, feature granularity, update frequencies) may necessitate multiple adapters or a more flexible schema.

Assumption: Versioning and audit trails are feasible without prohibitive storage or computational overhead. Risk: High-frequency updates in dynamic environments may generate large audit logs; retention policies and compression strategies are needed.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP5 to explicitly decouple feature detection from sensor modality by introducing an abstraction layer (e.g., 'normalized feature observations') that accepts outputs from multiple detection strategies. Add a safety validation gate post-conflict resolution to flag features requiring human review before model acceptance. Introduce a hierarchical spatial model (global + local frames) to support multi-zone and large-scale worksites. Define a versioning strategy that distinguishes between static reference geometry (infrequent updates, full audit) and dynamic features (frequent updates, summary audit). Clarify the query interface contract (e.g., spatial predicates, feature attributes, update latency SLAs) to enable independent consumer design.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized feature capture pipeline including raw sensor ingestion, feature detection, spatial registration, conflict resolution, persistent model store, standardized query interface, versioning, and audit trail management for safety compliance.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Raw sensor data ingestion from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems; feeds feature detection pipeline

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Audit trail and versioning metadata exported for safety compliance verification and incident reconstruction

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Query interface for downstream consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) to retrieve registered features and conflict-resolved model state

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Optional: distributed model synchronization and remote audit log persistence if multi-node deployment required

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE is the natural home for perception/understanding pipelines. Feature capture, detection, registration, and model persistence are core NGHE responsibilities. Conflict resolution and versioning align with NGHE's role as the central reasoning layer. Minor diffusion: audit trail export to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface is expected and minimal.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a consumer of features, not a producer/manager of the pipeline. Placing feature capture here would couple perception logic tightly to vehicle platform, preventing reuse across Teleoperation and other agents. High diffusion: query interface would need to be exposed externally; versioning/audit would leak into vehicle-specific code.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a perception pipeline owner. Feature capture here would be out of domain and create tight coupling between remote operation and model management. High diffusion: pipeline logic would be scattered across teleoperation command handlers; model store would be inaccessible to Vehicle and Safety Services without cross-module dependencies.

Weak fit: Safety Services is a consumer and validator of features, not the producer. While audit trails and versioning support safety compliance, the core feature capture pipeline (sensor ingestion, detection, registration, conflict resolution) is outside Safety Services' domain. High diffusion: feature detection logic would be misplaced; persistent model store would need to be accessed by Vehicle and Teleoperation, creating external dependencies.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The feature capture pipeline is fundamentally a perception and world-model management responsibility. NGHE Solution, as the central reasoning and understanding layer, is the natural owner. Raw sensor ingestion feeds NGHE's perception subsystem; feature detection and spatial registration are core NGHE functions; conflict resolution is a reasoning task; persistent model store is NGHE's internal state; versioning and audit trails support NGHE's transparency and safety compliance obligations. Downstream consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) query the model via the Tasking interface without owning the pipeline.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic module, the feature capture pipeline may grow too large to manage. Mitigation: decompose NGHE internally into sub-modules (e.g., PerceptionPipeline, ModelStore, QueryEngine) while keeping the IM boundary at NGHE Solution level.

Risk: Telemetry interface may become a bottleneck if multiple sensor types (camera, lidar, radar, etc.) feed the pipeline. Mitigation: design Telemetry as a multiplexed interface with schema versioning; consider a sensor abstraction layer within NGHE.

Risk: Audit trail and versioning may create storage/performance overhead. Mitigation: implement lazy audit logging and model snapshot strategies; use SafetyAlert interface to offload compliance-critical logs to Safety Services.

Risk: Conflict resolution logic may require domain-specific rules that vary by deployment. Mitigation: parameterize conflict resolution rules; expose them via Tasking interface for configuration by Safety Services or external policy engines.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 comprehensively addresses FR by specifying a complete pipeline (ingestion → detection → registration → conflict resolution → persistence) with explicit support for standardized query access, versioning, and audit trails. The DP is solution-agnostic (rule-based or learned classifiers) and directly maps to the functional requirement of capturing and representing worksite spatial features in machine-readable form. Safety compliance traceability is explicit.

diffusion_risks: Audit trail export via SafetyAlert interface creates a secondary responsibility boundary: NGHE owns pipeline logic and model persistence, but Safety Services owns compliance interpretation. Risk: audit log schema drift if Safety Services evolves independently. Mitigation: formalize SafetyAlert schema as a contract; version it explicitly.

Conflict resolution logic may require parameterization by external policies (worksite safety plan, operational policies per constraints). Risk: if conflict rules are hardcoded in NGHE, policy changes require NGHE redeployment. Mitigation: expose conflict resolution rules as queryable/configurable via Tasking interface; consider a policy engine sub-module within NGHE or external to it.

Telemetry interface multiplexing (camera, lidar, radar, etc.) could scatter sensor abstraction logic across NGHE if not carefully modularized. Risk: feature detection logic becomes sensor-specific rather than generic. Mitigation: enforce a sensor abstraction layer (e.g., PerceptionPipeline sub-module) that normalizes raw telemetry before feature detection.

Model store versioning and snapshot management may create tight coupling between query engine and persistence layer if not decoupled. Risk: query performance degrades with audit trail overhead. Mitigation: separate QueryEngine and ModelStore as internal NGHE sub-modules with explicit versioning API.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct IM owner (0.92 containment score). The pipeline is fundamentally a perception and world-model responsibility, not a vehicle control, teleoperation command, or safety validation function. All core DP elements (ingestion, detection, registration, conflict resolution, persistence) are contained within NGHE's domain. External interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert out, Tasking inout, Network Link optional) are well-defined and minimal. Internal decomposition into PerceptionPipeline, ModelStore, QueryEngine, and VersioningAudit sub-modules is recommended to prevent monolithic growth, but IM boundary remains at NGHE Solution level. No high-diffusion risks detected; all flagged risks are manageable via interface contracts and internal modularization.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria require that 'a worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions for an agreed set of site features.' NGHE Solution's Tasking interface directly satisfies this: downstream consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, planning services) query the persistent model store for registered features. Verification is testable: (1) confirm model generation from raw telemetry, (2) confirm query interface returns agreed feature set, (3) confirm versioning/audit trails support safety compliance audits. Worksite safety plan and operational policies can be validated by confirming conflict resolution rules are applied and audit trails record policy-driven decisions.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is unambiguously allocated ownership of the feature capture pipeline. Responsibilities are explicit: raw sensor ingestion (Telemetry interface), feature detection and spatial registration (internal PerceptionPipeline), conflict resolution (internal reasoning), persistent model store (internal ModelStore), standardized query interface (Tasking interface), versioning and audit trails (internal VersioningAudit). Downstream consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are clearly identified as query clients, not pipeline owners. No ambiguity in allocation; no competing ownership claims.

recommended_revisions: Formalize SafetyAlert interface schema as a versioned contract. Define audit log structure, compliance metadata, and incident reconstruction requirements explicitly. Establish change control between NGHE and Safety Services.

Decompose NGHE Solution internally into sub-modules: PerceptionPipeline (sensor abstraction + feature detection), ModelStore (persistent storage + versioning), QueryEngine (Tasking interface), VersioningAudit (audit trail management). Document internal interfaces and ownership of each sub-module.

Parameterize conflict resolution rules. Expose conflict resolution policy as a queryable/configurable artifact via Tasking interface or a dedicated PolicyConfiguration interface. Allow worksite safety plan and operational policies to be injected without NGHE redeployment.

Design Telemetry interface with sensor abstraction layer. Define a normalized sensor data schema that abstracts camera, lidar, radar, etc. Ensure feature detection logic operates on normalized data, not raw sensor-specific formats.
Implement lazy audit logging and model snapshot strategies to mitigate storage/performance overhead. Document retention policies and compliance requirements in SafetyAlert interface specification.

Add Network Link interface specification for multi-node deployment scenarios. Define model synchronization protocol, distributed versioning semantics, and remote audit log persistence requirements if applicable.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence (0.92 containment, high verification feasibility). However, the DP-IM gate identifies five material diffusion risks that require sub-module decomposition to prevent monolithic growth and ensure safety-critical audit trail integrity. The recommended revisions explicitly call for internal decomposition of NGHE Solution into PerceptionPipeline, ModelStore, QueryEngine, and VersioningAudit sub-modules. Given high safety criticality and the explicit need to formalize internal interfaces and conflict resolution parameterization, deriving next-level sub-FRs is justified to ensure architectural clarity, interface contracts, and risk mitigation before detailed design. Stopping now would defer critical architectural decisions (sensor abstraction, audit logging strategy, policy parameterization) to implementation, increasing rework risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Ingest and normalize heterogeneous sensor telemetry (camera, lidar, radar) into a unified feature-agnostic data representation suitable for downstream feature detection.
FR5.2: Detect and spatially register worksite features (obstacles, hazards, landmarks, boundaries) from normalized sensor data and maintain a persistent, versioned world model.

FR5.3: Resolve spatial and temporal conflicts in feature observations (e.g., duplicate detections, sensor disagreement, stale registrations) according to worksite safety plan and operational policies.
FR5.4: Provide standardized query access to the persistent world model, including feature retrieval, spatial queries, and version/snapshot selection for planning and execution consumers.

FR5.5: Generate, persist, and export audit trails of all feature detection, registration, conflict resolution, and query decisions to support safety compliance verification and incident reconstruction.

revise.why: DP5 is logically sound but lacks explicit specification of internal sub-module interfaces and conflict resolution parameterization. Revise DP5 to explicitly state: (1) sensor abstraction layer design (normalized schema for camera, lidar, radar), (2) conflict resolution rule parameterization mechanism (policy engine or queryable configuration), (3) audit trail schema and versioning contract with Safety Services, (4) lazy logging and snapshot strategies to mitigate storage overhead. These revisions ensure DP5 is not just solution-agnostic but also architecturally decomposable and safety-compliant before IM allocation.

assumptions: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are stable enough to be injected as configuration; they do not require real-time algorithmic adaptation.
Sensor telemetry streams (camera, lidar, radar) can be normalized into a common feature-agnostic representation without loss of safety-critical information.
Audit trail export via SafetyAlert interface is the primary compliance mechanism; no additional compliance reporting channels are required.
Query clients (Vehicle, Teleoperation, planning services) can tolerate versioned model snapshots; real-time model updates are not required for all queries.
NGHE Solution is the sole owner of the world model; no competing perception pipelines exist in the system.
open_risks: Audit trail schema drift: SafetyAlert interface contract must be formalized and versioned with Safety Services to prevent schema evolution misalignment. Mitigation: establish change control board; version SafetyAlert schema explicitly.

Conflict resolution policy coupling: if conflict rules are hardcoded in NGHE, policy changes require NGHE redeployment, creating operational friction. Mitigation: expose conflict resolution rules as queryable/configurable artifact; consider external policy engine.

Sensor abstraction leakage: feature detection logic may become sensor-specific if abstraction layer is not enforced. Mitigation: define normalized sensor data schema; enforce PerceptionPipeline sub-module boundary.
Model store performance degradation: audit trail overhead may impact query latency. Mitigation: implement lazy audit logging; separate QueryEngine and ModelStore with explicit versioning API.

Distributed deployment complexity: Network Link interface specification is deferred; multi-node model synchronization and distributed audit logging are not yet addressed. Mitigation: add Network Link interface specification if multi-node deployment is required.
evidence_needed: Sensor abstraction layer design: define normalized feature representation schema that abstracts camera, lidar, radar inputs. Validate that feature detection logic can operate on normalized data without sensor-specific branching.

Conflict resolution parameterization: specify mechanism for injecting worksite safety plan and operational policies (e.g., configuration file, policy engine, Tasking interface extension). Validate that policy changes do not require NGHE redeployment.
SafetyAlert interface contract: formalize audit log schema, compliance metadata, and incident reconstruction requirements. Establish versioning and change control with Safety Services.
Audit trail performance analysis: measure storage and query latency overhead of audit logging. Validate that lazy logging and snapshot strategies meet performance SLAs.

Sub-module interface specifications: document internal interfaces between PerceptionPipeline, ModelStore, QueryEngine, and VersioningAudit. Validate that internal boundaries prevent monolithic growth and enable independent testing.

Safety compliance verification plan: define acceptance criteria for audit trail completeness, feature detection accuracy, and conflict resolution correctness. Validate that NGHE Solution's Tasking interface and audit trails support compliance audits.
